statusLine: European fuel security remains under strain with 20 of 27 countries holding critical reserves in at least one fuel type, despite EU-wide averages appearing stable.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel stocks present a concerning picture beneath surface-level stability. While EU averages show petrol at 96.3 days and diesel at 88.8 days, these figures mask significant disparities across member states. A total of 20 of 27 countries currently maintain critical reserve levels in at least one fuel category, with Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal experiencing critical shortages across all three fuel types. The 90-day mandatory minimum for net imports serves as a crucial benchmark that many countries are failing to meet.

Jet fuel stocks present the most acute challenge, averaging just 87.3 days EU-wide and falling below the 90-day threshold. Countries like Poland (22.5 days), Portugal (21.2 days), and Finland (31.9 days) face particularly severe jet fuel shortages. This pattern reflects both the specialized nature of aviation fuel supply chains and the ongoing impact of Middle East supply disruptions that have prompted strategic reserve releases across the region.

Brent crude prices have surged 26.6% since late February to $91.78 per barrel, reflecting persistent supply tensions. This price pressure is evident in retail fuel costs, with petrol averaging €1.786 per liter and notable regional variations. Denmark leads at €2.392 per liter while Malta offers the lowest prices at €1.34 per liter, highlighting the diverse impact of supply constraints and national fuel policies across the EU.

The data reveals a fuel security landscape under significant pressure, with critical shortages concentrated in Central and Eastern European countries, alongside traditional importers like Luxembourg and Portugal. While some nations like Estonia, Latvia, and Ireland maintain comfortable reserves, the widespread nature of critical-level stocks across 20 countries signals systemic strain in European fuel security infrastructure.
keyPoints: 20 of 27 EU countries maintain critical fuel reserves in at least one category, with 5 countries critical across all fuel types
Jet fuel presents the greatest vulnerability at 87.3 days average, falling below the 90-day mandatory minimum
Central and Eastern European nations show particularly acute shortages, with Poland holding just 22.5 days of jet fuel
Brent crude has risen 26.6% since late February to $91.78/barrel, driving retail petrol prices to €1.786 average
Regional price disparities span €1.05 per liter for petrol, from Malta's €1.34 to Denmark's €2.392
